python -c "
content = open(r'data/read_file.txt', encoding='utf-8').read()
english = '''Summer always arrives with a passionate and radiant presence. Unlike spring, it is not reserved; unlike autumn, it is not tranquil; and unlike winter, it is not cold. The coming of summer often begins on a certain morning: the leaves outside the window suddenly turn deep green, sunlight falls on the windowsill earlier than the day before, and the air carries a hint of humid warmth. Before people fully realize it, the season has quietly changed its appearance. The plane trees along the street spread their enormous green canopies, and cicadas tentatively begin their songs among the branches and leaves, as if announcing that a grand and prolonged bustle is about to begin. The world of summer seems to have its brightness turned upˇŞthe sky is bluer, the clouds whiter, and even the distant buildings and roads appear remarkably clear.

The days of summer are long, and the sunlight is exceptionally abundant. The morning sun is still gentle, casting a pale golden sheen over playgrounds, rooftops, and river surfaces; but by noon, the sun reveals its full power without reserve, baking the ground until it scorches and heating the air until it shimmers. A transparent heat wave seems to rise from the asphalt road, dogs hide in the shade panting with tongues out, and pedestrians quicken their steps, eager to step into rooms with fans or air conditioning. Yet even so, the summer heat is not merely irritatingˇŞit also makes all things appear full of vitality. Vegetation grows wildly, flowers bloom in full splendor, lotus leaves stretch from one end of the pond to the other, and lotus flowers emerge from the emerald green, pink and white, fresh and elegant. That vigorous sense of growth is the most distinctive undertone of summer.

Summer is also the season richest in sounds. There are bird songs in the morning, cicadas in the afternoon, children laughing in the evening, and insects chirping in the grass at night. Especially the cicadasˇŞtheir voices have almost become an indispensable backdrop of summer. They hide on unseen treetops, singing tirelessly, their voices rising wave after wave, as if they want to sing the entire season to its most passionate peak. When I was young, I always found the cicadas noisy; but as I grew older, I came to feel that they are a memory unique to summer. Whenever I hear their songs, I am reminded of summer vacation daysˇŞof chilled watermelon, bamboo-mat naps, sunset clouds in the evening, and the figures of me and my friends chasing each other at the alley entrance. Summer seems inherently connected to childhood, because the memories it gives us are always bright, free, and seemingly endless.

Of course, the most enchanting moments of summer are often not at noon, but in the evening. The sun gradually sinks in the west, its scorching light softens, and the sky is dyed orange-red, rose purple, and pale gold. People who have been busy all day step out of their homesˇŞthe elderly sit under trees waving palm-leaf fans, children run across the square, and stalls selling cold jelly, popsicles, and fruit are surrounded by crowds. The evening breeze blows from deep within the alleys, carrying the scents of earth, grass, and cooking food, gently brushing against one's cheeks. At this time, the irritability accumulated during the day seems to slowly dissipate, and both city and countryside become soft. If one takes a walk by the river, one can see the sunset reflected on the water, with dragonflies occasionally skimming across the surface, sending ripples outward in concentric circles, as if time itself has been stretched.

Summer nights possess another kind of quiet beauty. The bustle of the day gradually fades, and nightfall spreads like a deep blue veil, gently covering the earth. The moon rises, stars appear one by one, and although residual warmth lingers in the air, it no longer overwhelms. Some sit on balconies to cool off, some hold half a watermelon while watching television, and others stroll slowly along the streets. Occasionally, the sound of a car passing by drifts from afar, while nearby, wind rustles through the leaves. In the countryside, summer nights feel even more vast: frogs croak across the fields, fireflies flicker on and off among the grass, and night wind sweeps through the rice paddies like a green wave. Such nights require few wordsˇŞsimply sitting quietly, one can feel the steadiness and tenderness of life itself.

Summer is passionate, yet fleeting. It makes people sweat and tire, but it also lets them feel the fullest state of life. It has blinding sunlight and cool post-rain freshness; noisy cicadas and tranquil starry skies; the scorching heat of day and the gentleness of evening. Perhaps precisely because summer is so intense, it is more easily preserved in people's memories. Many years later, we may forget exactly what happened in a particular summer, yet we will still remember that feeling: sunlight falling on our shoulders, wind threading through leaves, cold soda fizzing in our palms, and someone laughing and calling our name from afar. In that moment, summer is not merely a seasonˇŞit is more like a bright passage of time, passionate, free, and full of hope.'''
with open(r'data/english.txt', 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    f.write(english)
print('Done')
"
